bibliographic_data.title: Aneurysm Characteristics, Study Population, and Endovascular Techniques for the Treatment of Intracranial Aneurysms in a Large, Prospective, Multicenter Cohort: Results of the Analysis of Recanalization after Endovascular Treatment of Intracranial Aneurysm Study
bibliographic_data.abstract: BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE: The Analysis of Recanalization after Endovascular Treatment of Intracranial Aneurysm (ARETA) prospective study aims to determine factors predicting recurrence after endovascular treatment for intracranial aneurysms. In this publication, we review endovascular techniques and present the study population. Characteristics of treated and untreated unruptured aneurysms were analyzed.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study provides insights into current neurointerventional practices for treating ruptured and unruptured intracranial aneurysms, focusing on the choice of endovascular techniques based on aneurysm characteristics.
core_content.key_methodology: Prospective multicenter cohort study enrolling patients treated between December 2013 and May 2015. Patient demographics, aneurysm characteristics, and endovascular techniques were recorded.
outcomes: Ruptured intracranial aneurysms were treated by coiling and balloon-assisted coiling in 97.8% of cases.
In unruptured intracranial aneurysms, the rates of flow diversion, flow disruption, and stent-assisted coiling were 11.6%, 6.9%, and 7.8%, respectively.
Rupture status, aneurysm location, neck diameter, and sac size significantly influenced the chosen technique.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The study aimed to determine factors predicting recurrence after endovascular treatment for intracranial aneurysms by reviewing endovascular techniques and presenting the characteristics of treated and untreated unruptured aneurysms. A total of 1289 patients with 1761 intracranial aneurysms, 835 (47.4%) ruptured, were enrolled in a large, prospective, multicenter cohort study between December 2013 and May 2015. The techniques used for treatment included coiling and balloon-assisted coiling for ruptured aneurysms, while flow diversion, flow disruption, and stent-assisted coiling were commonly applied to unruptured aneurysms. Factors such as rupture status, aneurysm location, neck diameter, and sac size significantly influenced the choice of technique.
